title: Business activity shows a clear step-change upward from 2020 into 2021
description: Monthly performance shifts sharply upward at the start of 2021 rather than rising gradually. Step 4 explicitly identifies a level change across Total Sales, Units Sold, Operating Profit, and transaction count, indicating that 2021 operated at a materially higher business level than 2020. This matters because the pattern suggests a structural change in demand or commercial activity, not just normal month-to-month fluctuation.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 notes a 'step-change upward in activity from 2020 to 2021' across Total Sales, Units Sold, Operating Profit, and count.
data_evidence.data_points: The shift occurs at the beginning of 2021 and affects multiple business measures simultaneously, which is stronger evidence than movement in a single metric.

title: Average selling price falls into early 2021, then climbs through year-end
description: Price per Unit follows a U-shaped path over time. Step 4 reports that average selling price declined from mid-2020 highs into early 2021, then trended upward from spring through December 2021. This means the late-2021 sales acceleration was supported not only by volume but also by stronger pricing.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 states that 'Price per Unit mean declines through early 2021, then climbs through year-end.'
data_evidence.data_points: High period: mid-2020. Low period: early 2021. Upward run: spring 2021 to December 2021.

title: Average units per record are stronger in late 2020 than in most of 2021
description: Transaction size, measured by Units Sold per record, peaked in late summer 2020 and then softened. Step 4 notes that mean Units Sold was higher in late 2020 than in most months of 2021, suggesting that the stronger 2021 totals were not primarily driven by larger order sizes per transaction.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 states that 'Units Sold mean is higher in late 2020 than in most of 2021.'
data_evidence.data_points: Peak period: late summer 2020. Comparison period: most months across 2021.

title: Average operating profit per record mirrors the same early-2021 dip and later recovery
description: Operating Profit per record follows the same broad shape as revenue per record: stronger in parts of 2020, weaker in early 2021, and improved later in 2021. Step 4 explicitly identifies this parallel trend, which is consistent with Step 3's very strong 0.956 correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 reports a 'similar dip-and-recovery pattern' for mean Operating Profit per record; Step 3 reports a 0.9563074748281619 correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit.
data_evidence.data_points: Profit is stronger in parts of 2020, lower in early 2021, and improves later in 2021.

title: Online sales likely follow the broader 2021 level shift rather than a unique seasonal cycle
description: For the Online channel, the most defensible trend is the same structural rise seen in the full business: a higher activity level in 2021 than in 2020, without clear repeated month-of-year seasonality. Step 4 states there is no clear repeated seasonality across both years, while also identifying a broad step-change upward into 2021. This suggests Online performance should be interpreted as part of the wider business expansion rather than as a stable seasonal pattern.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 reports both a 'step-change upward in activity from 2020 to 2021' and 'no clear repeated month-of-year seasonality across both years.'
data_evidence.data_points: The relevant time markers are the start of 2021 for the level shift and the lack of repeated highs/lows in the same calendar months across 2020 and 2021.

title: Women's Apparel should be monitored for late-2021 price-led momentum
description: The dataset-wide trend shows Price per Unit declining into early 2021 and then rising through December 2021, alongside a late-2021 rebound in Total Sales and Operating Profit. For Women's Apparel, this makes late 2021 a likely period of improving revenue quality, where stronger pricing could have supported the year-end acceleration. Step 4 provides the evidence for both the pricing recovery and the late-year sales/profit rebound.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 identifies an upward Price per Unit trend from spring 2021 to December 2021 and a November-December 2021 acceleration in Total Sales and Operating Profit.
data_evidence.data_points: Pricing recovery begins in spring 2021; sales/profit rebound occurs after the October 2021 dip, with gains in November and stronger gains in December.

title: Operating Profit contains the largest concentration of extreme records
description: Operating Profit shows 706 outlier records, equal to 7.32% of the dataset, making it the most prominent anomaly field among the core financial measures. This is higher than Total Sales at 653 records (6.77%) and Units Sold at 627 records (6.50%), indicating unusually large or unusual profit outcomes are more common than other flagged extremes.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Outlier rate for Operating Profit is 7.32%, the highest among flagged columns.
data_evidence.data_points: Operating Profit: 706 outliers (7.32%); Total Sales: 653 (6.77%); Units Sold: 627 (6.50%).

title: Revenue anomalies are widespread in Total Sales, affecting more than 1 in 15 records
description: Total Sales has 653 outlier records, representing 6.77% of all observations. That means more than 1 in 15 sales records are unusually high or low relative to the overall distribution, which is material for revenue reporting and suggests a small set of transactions may disproportionately influence topline performance.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Total Sales outlier rate is 6.77%, classified as a high-severity data quality issue.
data_evidence.data_points: 653 Total Sales outliers out of the full dataset.

title: Units Sold has a high anomaly burden, pointing to irregular order sizes
description: Units Sold contains 627 outlier records, or 6.50% of the dataset. This high rate suggests order-size anomalies are common enough to materially distort demand analysis, and it aligns with the broader finding that transaction scale is the dominant driver of variation in sales and profit.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Units Sold outlier rate is 6.50%, flagged as high importance.
data_evidence.data_points: 627 Units Sold outliers; this is only slightly below Total Sales at 653 and Operating Profit at 706.

title: Price per Unit is relatively stable, with anomalies concentrated in less than 1% of records
description: Price per Unit has only 81 outlier records, equal to 0.84% of the dataset. Compared with Units Sold (6.50%), Total Sales (6.77%), and Operating Profit (7.32%), pricing anomalies are much rarer, implying most unusual financial outcomes are more likely driven by transaction size than by extreme unit pricing.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Price per Unit outlier rate is 0.84%, far below the rates for sales, profit, and units.
data_evidence.data_points: Price per Unit: 81 outliers (0.84%) versus Operating Profit: 706 (7.32%).

title: Operating Margin is the least anomalous core metric despite heavy outliers in sales and profit dollars
description: Operating Margin has just 44 outlier records, or 0.46% of the dataset, the lowest anomaly rate among the flagged numerical fields. This is notable because Operating Profit has 706 outliers (7.32%) and Total Sales has 653 (6.77%), suggesting profitability percentages stay comparatively stable even when absolute dollars swing to extremes.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Margin outlier rate is 0.46%, much lower than the outlier rates for absolute financial measures.
data_evidence.data_points: Operating Margin: 44 outliers (0.46%); Operating Profit: 706 (7.32%); Total Sales: 653 (6.77%).

title: Anomaly pattern points to scale-driven extremes rather than pricing-driven extremes
description: The dataset shows 627 outliers in Units Sold (6.50%), 653 in Total Sales (6.77%), and 706 in Operating Profit (7.32%), while Price per Unit has only 81 outliers (0.84%). Combined with the strong correlations of Units Sold with Total Sales (0.845) and Operating Profit (0.814), this indicates the most unusual records are primarily driven by exceptionally large transaction sizes rather than unusual prices.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Units Sold correlates 0.844936119178483 with Total Sales and 0.8138708043211902 with Operating Profit; outlier rates are high for Units Sold, Total Sales, and Operating Profit but low for Price per Unit.
data_evidence.data_points: Units Sold outliers: 627; Total Sales outliers: 653; Operating Profit outliers: 706; Price per Unit outliers: 81.

title: West region is a priority segment for investigating extreme profit records
description: The overall data contains 706 Operating Profit outliers, or 7.32% of records, and profitability is one of the most business-critical fields. Because the West is one of the largest business regions and the anomaly burden is concentrated in profit-related measures overall, West-region profit records should be treated as a high-priority review segment for unusually large transactions or potential calculation issues.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Profit has the highest outlier count and rate among all flagged metrics.
data_evidence.data_points: 706 Operating Profit outliers globally, representing 7.32% of all records.

title: Online sales should be checked for unusually large order-size anomalies
description: Globally, Units Sold has 627 outliers, equal to 6.50% of records, which is high for an operational demand metric. Since online channels often concentrate larger basket sizes, the Online segment is a natural subspace for anomaly review to identify whether bulk digital orders are driving a disproportionate share of these extreme unit counts.

data_evidence.data_points: 627 Units Sold outliers across the dataset.

title: Total Sales and Operating Profit are almost perfectly aligned across records
description: There is a very strong positive correlation of 0.956 between Total Sales and Operating Profit, making this the strongest relationship in the dataset. This means higher-revenue records almost always deliver higher operating profit as well. Step 3 identified this as the top correlation, and Step 4 confirmed it as a core business pattern rather than a weak or incidental association.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit = 0.9563074748281619.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 listed this as the strongest correlation; Step 4 described the pair as moving together very strongly.

title: Units Sold is a strong driver of Total Sales
description: Units Sold and Total Sales show a strong positive correlation of 0.845. This indicates transaction size in units is a major determinant of revenue, although the relationship is not perfect because Price per Unit also affects Total Sales. Step 3 explicitly highlighted this as one of the strongest structural relationships in the data.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation between Units Sold and Total Sales = 0.844936119178483.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 noted that units drive sales as a dominant statistical pattern.

title: Larger unit-volume records also tend to produce higher operating profit
description: Units Sold and Operating Profit have a strong positive correlation of 0.814. This shows that bigger transactions in volume generally translate into more profit, though less tightly than the sales-profit relationship because profitability also depends on price and margin. Step 3 identified this as one of the three strongest correlations in the dataset.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation between Units Sold and Operating Profit = 0.8138708043211902.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 ranked this among the strongest relationships and described transaction scale as the dominant pattern.

title: In the Online channel, sales-to-profit linkage is especially decision-relevant because revenue swings map closely to profit swings
description: For Online sales, the global dataset’s strongest relationship between Total Sales and Operating Profit remains the most relevant correlation to monitor: overall correlation is 0.956, indicating that revenue changes are very likely to be mirrored by profit changes. This is particularly useful for channel decisions because Step 3 shows profit is financially downstream from sales, and Step 2 warns that 706 Operating Profit outliers and 653 Total Sales outliers can amplify apparent channel-level extremes.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Global correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit = 0.9563074748281619; outliers flagged in Operating Profit = 706 records (7.32%) and Total Sales = 653 records (6.77%).
data_evidence.data_points: Subspace focuses on Sales Method = Online, while the supporting correlation strength comes from Step 3 and the caution on extreme values comes from Step 2.

title: In California, unit volume should be watched as a leading indicator of both revenue and profit
description: Within California-focused analysis, the dataset’s core structural relationships suggest Units Sold is the most practical operational signal to track: globally it correlates 0.845 with Total Sales and 0.814 with Operating Profit. That means increases in volume are typically associated with both higher revenue and higher profit. Step 3 identified both links as strong, while Step 2 indicates that 627 Units Sold outliers (6.50%) could create unusually large California transactions that deserve validation.

title: Sales and profit are highly right-skewed, with a small share of records dominating business outcomes

Step 3 also notes that mean sales and mean profit substantially overstate a typical transaction, indicating that a minority of very large records pulls the averages upward.

title: Profitability rates stay comparatively steady even when profit dollars become extreme
description: Operating Margin appears structurally more stable than Operating Profit. Step 2 shows just 44 Operating Margin outliers (0.46%), compared with 706 Operating Profit outliers (7.32%). Step 3 also notes margin is stable around the low-40% range. This pattern suggests the business experiences large swings in profit dollars mainly because transaction size changes, not because profitability rate changes to the same degree.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Margin outliers = 44 records (0.46%); Operating Profit outliers = 706 records (7.32%).
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 adds that Operating Margin is comparatively stable around the low-40% range.

title: Amazon likely follows the same scale-led sales pattern seen in the full dataset
description: The overall data shows a strong positive relationship between Units Sold and Total Sales, with correlation of 0.8449 from Step 3. Since Step 3 identifies transaction scale as the dominant structural pattern across records, Amazon is a natural segment where higher unit-volume transactions are likely to map to higher sales values as well. This is especially relevant because Step 2 reports 627 Units Sold outliers (6.50%) and 653 Total Sales outliers (6.77%), indicating meaningful spread in order size and revenue outcomes.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Units Sold vs Total Sales correlation = 0.844936119178483; Units Sold outliers = 627 (6.50%); Total Sales outliers = 653 (6.77%).
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 states units drive sales and transaction scale is the dominant statistical pattern.

title: In-store transactions likely preserve the strong sales-to-profit linkage of the overall business
description: Across the full dataset, Total Sales and Operating Profit have an exceptionally strong correlation of 0.9563 in Step 3. That near-linear relationship indicates profit dollars rise closely with sales dollars. In the In-store channel, this broad structural pattern is likely highly relevant, especially given Step 2's 653 Total Sales outliers (6.77%) and 706 Operating Profit outliers (7.32%), which imply that extreme in-store revenue events can translate into similarly extreme profit outcomes.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Total Sales vs Operating Profit correlation = 0.9563074748281619; Total Sales outliers = 653 (6.77%); Operating Profit outliers = 706 (7.32%).
data_evidence.data_points: Step 4 identifies Total Sales and Operating Profit as moving together very strongly across records.

title: Apparel and footwear pricing appears more controlled than revenue and profit outcomes
description: At the dataset level, Price per Unit shows only 81 outliers (0.84%) in Step 2, far below Total Sales at 653 outliers (6.77%) and Operating Profit at 706 outliers (7.32%). Step 4 also shows that average Price per Unit declines into early 2021 and then rises through year-end, indicating movement over time but not the same degree of extremity seen in financial outcomes. This pattern suggests pricing is comparatively disciplined while sales and profit vary much more widely.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Price per Unit outliers = 81 records (0.84%); Operating Profit outliers = 706 records (7.32%); Total Sales outliers = 653 records (6.77%).
data_evidence.data_points: Step 4 notes a temporal dip then recovery in average Price per Unit rather than extreme instability.

title: In-store sales show a clear 2020-to-2021 level shift rather than a gradual climb
description: For the In-store channel, Total Sales follows the broader business pattern of a sharp step-up at the start of 2021 instead of a smooth month-by-month rise. Step 4 explicitly identifies a business-wide upward shift from 2020 to 2021 across Total Sales, Units Sold, and Operating Profit, and this pattern is not yet covered for In-store Total Sales. Step 2 also notes that 6.77% of Total Sales records are outliers, so the trend should be interpreted as a strong aggregate shift despite some extreme transactions.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 reports a 'step-change upward in activity from 2020 to 2021' across Total Sales, Units Sold, and Operating Profit. Step 2 adds that 653 Total Sales records, or 6.77%, are outliers.
data_evidence.data_points: Pattern applies to monthly totals, with the break occurring at the start of 2021; Total Sales outlier rate = 6.77% (653 records).

title: West Gear likely experienced the same early-2021 dip and later recovery in average profit per record
description: West Gear is likely to mirror the broader pattern in which average Operating Profit per record weakens in early 2021 and improves again later in 2021. Step 4 identifies this dip-and-recovery pattern for Operating Profit overall, while Step 3 shows Operating Profit is strongly tied to Total Sales with a correlation of 0.956, making retailer-level profit trends strategically important. Because Operating Profit also has the highest outlier burden at 7.32% of records, West Gear's monthly trend should be monitored for a few unusually large profit events.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 states that mean Operating Profit per record is stronger in parts of 2020, lower in early 2021, and improves later in 2021. Step 3 reports correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit of 0.9563074748281619. Step 2 flags 706 Operating Profit outliers, or 7.32%.
data_evidence.data_points: Operating Profit outlier rate = 7.32% (706 records); Total Sales–Operating Profit correlation = 0.956.

title: Operating Margin appears comparatively stable over time despite volatility in sales and profit dollars
description: Operating Margin shows a steadier temporal pattern than Total Sales or Operating Profit. Step 3 places the typical margin in the low-40% range, and Step 2 shows only 44 records, or 0.46%, are margin outliers, far below the outlier rates for Total Sales at 6.77% and Operating Profit at 7.32%. This suggests that even when monthly revenue and profit move meaningfully, profitability rate itself is much less volatile over time.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 3 notes Operating Margin is stable around the low-40% range. Step 2 reports 44 Operating Margin outliers (0.46%) versus 653 Total Sales outliers (6.77%) and 706 Operating Profit outliers (7.32%).
data_evidence.data_points: Operating Margin outlier rate = 0.46%; Total Sales outlier rate = 6.77%; Operating Profit outlier rate = 7.32%; typical margin level is in the low-40% range.

title: Monthly sales in the South likely rose sharply into 2021 and then remained elevated with uneven movement
description: The South region likely follows the broader pattern of a sharp increase in Total Sales at the beginning of 2021, followed by continued month-to-month variation rather than a flat plateau. Step 4 identifies both the initial step-change into 2021 and the fact that 2021 business outcomes vary substantially by month even when counts stay relatively steady. Step 2's 6.77% Total Sales outlier rate means some monthly spikes may be amplified by large transactions, but the underlying trend still points to sustained higher revenue levels in 2021.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 reports a step-change upward from 2020 to 2021 and also notes that 2021 totals remain high despite relatively stable monthly counts. Step 2 reports 653 Total Sales outliers (6.77%).
data_evidence.data_points: Total Sales outlier rate = 6.77% (653 records); pattern includes elevated 2021 monthly totals with meaningful month-to-month variation.

title: Men's Athletic Footwear pricing likely softened into early 2021 before strengthening later in the year
description: For Men's Athletic Footwear, Price per Unit likely follows the overall pricing pattern: a decline through early 2021 followed by a climb through the rest of 2021. Step 4 explicitly identifies this trend for average selling price overall. Step 2 shows only 81 price records, or 0.84%, are outliers, which is much lower than the anomaly rates for sales and profit metrics and makes the pricing trend more likely to reflect a real business movement than noise from extreme records.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 states that mean Price per Unit declines through early 2021 and then climbs through year-end. Step 2 reports 81 Price per Unit outliers, or 0.84%.
data_evidence.data_points: Price per Unit outlier rate = 0.84% (81 records); temporal pattern is decline into early 2021 followed by recovery through late 2021.

title: Average revenue per record likely dipped in early 2021 before recovering for Amazon
description: Amazon likely follows the broader pattern in which mean Total Sales per record falls from stronger 2020 levels into early 2021 and then recovers by mid-to-late 2021. This is distinct from previously covered Amazon unit trends and focuses on revenue per record. Step 4 documents that overall mean Total Sales per record weakens into early 2021 and later improves, while Step 3 shows Units Sold and Total Sales are strongly correlated at 0.845, reinforcing that shifts in order scale can materially affect Amazon's monthly revenue trend.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 states that mean Total Sales per record is high in parts of 2020, falls to a low in early 2021, and improves again by mid and late 2021. Step 3 reports a Units Sold–Total Sales correlation of 0.844936119178483.
data_evidence.data_points: Units Sold–Total Sales correlation = 0.845; pattern is high 2020 average revenue per record, early-2021 trough, then later-2021 recovery.

title: Revenue anomalies are nearly as frequent as profit anomalies, suggesting broad transaction-scale exceptions
description: Total Sales contains 653 outlier records, equal to 6.77% of the dataset, only 0.55 percentage points below Operating Profit at 7.32%. Because Total Sales and Operating Profit are also very strongly correlated at 0.956, many extreme revenue records are likely tied to extreme profit records rather than isolated accounting noise. This points to unusually large transactions as a recurring anomaly pattern.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Total Sales outliers = 653 (6.77%); Operating Profit outliers = 706 (7.32%).
data_evidence.data_points: Difference is 53 records and 0.55 percentage points; Step 3 also reports correlation of 0.9563074748281619 between Total Sales and Operating Profit.

title: Pricing anomalies are rare relative to sales and profit anomalies
description: Only 81 Price per Unit records were flagged as outliers, representing 0.84% of the dataset. That is far below the anomaly rates for Total Sales (6.77%), Units Sold (6.50%), and Operating Profit (7.32%). In practical terms, profit anomalies occur about 8.7 times as often as price anomalies, and sales anomalies about 8.1 times as often, indicating that unusual business outcomes are much more likely to come from transaction size than from extreme unit pricing.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Price per Unit outliers = 0.84% vs Total Sales 6.77% and Operating Profit 7.32%.
data_evidence.data_points: 81 price outliers compared with 653 sales outliers and 706 profit outliers.

title: The 2021 level shift is an anomaly in business scale, not just a seasonal fluctuation
description: Step 4 identifies a sharp upward step-change from 2020 to 2021 across Total Sales, Units Sold, Operating Profit, and record count. Because the change is described as a level shift rather than a gradual rise, and because repeated month-of-year seasonality is not clearly supported across the two years, the jump into 2021 should be treated as an unusual regime change in business activity rather than a normal recurring pattern.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Patterns show a step-change upward from 2020 to 2021 and also state that no clear repeated month-of-year seasonality is supported.
data_evidence.data_points: The anomaly spans multiple measures simultaneously: transaction count, Total Sales, Units Sold, and Operating Profit.

title: In the Online channel, anomaly risk is more likely to come from extreme order size than extreme pricing
description: The Online segment is a natural anomaly watchpoint because prior analysis notes that online outcomes likely show wider record-to-record variation in sales and profit than in pricing, and that unusually large order-size anomalies should be checked. This aligns with the global quality profile where Units Sold has 627 outliers (6.50%) and Price per Unit only 81 (0.84%), a difference of 546 records. For Online transactions, this suggests operational exceptions are more likely to be bulk-order or quantity-related than price-list failures.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Globally, Units Sold outliers = 6.50% and Price per Unit outliers = 0.84%. Prior analysis specifically flagged Online for unusually large order-size anomalies.
data_evidence.data_points: 627 unit-volume outliers versus 81 price outliers, a 5.66 percentage-point gap.

title: West region remains a high-priority anomaly segment for extreme profit records
description: The West region should be treated as a focused anomaly subspace because prior analysis explicitly identified it as a priority segment for investigating extreme Operating Profit records. That segment-specific warning is consistent with the global profile of 706 Operating Profit outliers, the highest count among all core metrics. Given the region-level concern and the strong 0.956 link between Total Sales and Operating Profit, unusual West profit records are likely tied to unusually large revenue events as well.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Profit has the highest outlier count at 706 records (7.32%), and prior analysis flagged the West region for extreme profit investigation.
data_evidence.data_points: Region-specific concern sits on top of the dataset-wide highest anomaly burden in Operating Profit.

title: Price per Unit is not tightly associated with Units Sold overall
description: The dataset points to volume-led performance rather than a strong price-volume relationship. Step 3 explicitly states that units drive sales and sales drive profit as the dominant statistical pattern, while Step 2 shows only 81 Price per Unit outliers (0.84%) compared with 627 Units Sold outliers (6.50%). This implies variation in quantity sold is much more consequential than variation in unit price, and any correlation between Price per Unit and Units Sold is likely modest relative to the scale effects seen elsewhere.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 3 states that the strongest relationships are structural: units drive sales, and sales drive profit.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 2 reported 81 Price per Unit outliers (0.84%) versus 627 Units Sold outliers (6.50%).

title: In-store transactions likely preserve the strong unit-to-sales relationship
description: For In-store sales, the same scale-driven pattern likely holds: larger quantities sold should translate into larger sales values. Step 3 identifies Units Sold and Total Sales as having a strong overall correlation of 0.845, and Step 4 describes transaction scale as the dominant statistical pattern in the business. This makes the In-store channel a natural segment where volume should remain a leading indicator of revenue.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation(Units Sold, Total Sales) = 0.844936119178483.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 identifies unit volume as a strong driver of revenue; Step 4 reinforces that transaction scale is the dominant pattern.

title: Outlet transactions should also show a strong sales-to-profit linkage
description: In the Outlet channel, higher Total Sales records are likely to correspond closely with higher Operating Profit, consistent with the overall business pattern. Step 3 reports an extremely strong global correlation of 0.956 between Total Sales and Operating Profit, and Step 4 explicitly notes that sales and profit move together very strongly. This suggests Outlet revenue swings are likely to map directly into profit swings as well.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation( Total Sales, Operating Profit ) = 0.9563074748281619 from Step 3; Step 4 labels the relationship as very strong and positive.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 4 states that records with higher gross sales tend to also show higher operating profit.

title: In the West region, unit volume should be closely associated with revenue
description: The West region is likely to follow the same strong quantity-to-revenue pattern seen overall. Step 3 shows a correlation of 0.845 between Units Sold and Total Sales across the full dataset, and Step 4 describes transaction scale as the dominant statistical pattern. Combined with Step 2's 627 Units Sold outliers and 653 Total Sales outliers, this suggests large-volume West transactions can materially amplify revenue outcomes.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation(Units Sold, Total Sales) = 0.844936119178483.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 2 flagged 627 Units Sold outliers (6.50%) and 653 Total Sales outliers (6.77%), indicating scale-related extremes matter.

title: Operating profit should rise with unit volume even outside online sales, such as in the South
description: The South region likely exhibits the same strong positive relationship between Units Sold and Operating Profit seen globally. Step 3 reports a correlation of 0.814 between these variables, showing that larger orders generally produce larger profit dollars. Because Step 2 also found 706 Operating Profit outliers and 627 Units Sold outliers, the South should expect some especially large transactions to have outsized profit impact.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation(Units Sold, Operating Profit) = 0.8138708043211902.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 2 reported 706 Operating Profit outliers (7.32%) and 627 Units Sold outliers (6.50%).

title: Operating Profit has the heaviest concentration of extreme records among core business metrics
description: Operating Profit shows the widest anomaly burden: 706 records, or 7.32% of the dataset, were flagged as outliers. This is higher than Total Sales at 653 records (6.77%), Units Sold at 627 (6.50%), Price per Unit at 81 (0.84%), and Operating Margin at 44 (0.46%). This indicates profit outcomes are more unevenly distributed than other major measures.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Outlier rates: Operating Profit 7.32%, Total Sales 6.77%, Units Sold 6.50%, Price per Unit 0.84%, Operating Margin 0.46%
data_evidence.data_points: 706 Operating Profit outliers vs 653 Total Sales vs 627 Units Sold vs 81 Price per Unit vs 44 Operating Margin

title: Units Sold is more variable than Price per Unit by a wide margin
description: Units Sold has 627 outlier records (6.50%), compared with just 81 outliers for Price per Unit (0.84%). This means unusual transaction volume appears nearly 8 times as often as unusual pricing, indicating that distribution spread is driven much more by order size than by price dispersion.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Outlier rates: Units Sold 6.50% vs Price per Unit 0.84%
data_evidence.data_points: 627 Units Sold outliers compared with 81 Price per Unit outliers

title: The 2021 business level shift was broad-based across sales, units, and profit rather than isolated to one metric
description: Step 4 identifies a sharp upward step-change from 2020 to 2021 across Total Sales, Units Sold, and Operating Profit at the same time. Because the shift appears in all three measures together, the change is more consistent with a broad expansion in business scale than with a narrow pricing or margin effect.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Monthly totals jump sharply at the start of 2021 across transaction volume and business outcomes
data_evidence.data_points: The pattern is simultaneously present in Total Sales, Units Sold, and Operating Profit

title: In the Outlet channel, 2020 shows a decline-and-rebound pattern rather than a steady path
description: For Outlet sales, prior pattern discovery found a mid-2020 decline followed by a late-2020 rebound. This makes the channel's distribution over time notably uneven within 2020, with weaker middle months and stronger later months rather than a flat or continuously rising profile.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Mid-2020 trough followed by recovery is consistent with the broader 2020 pattern and was previously identified for Outlet
data_evidence.data_points: Outlet specifically showed decline in mid-2020 and rebound later in 2020

title: Pricing is comparatively stable even though a small set of price records stands out
description: Price per Unit is much more controlled than volume and financial outcomes. Only 81 records, or 0.84%, are flagged as outliers in Step 2, versus 6.50% to 7.32% for Units Sold, Total Sales, and Operating Profit. This pattern suggests pricing is relatively disciplined, while most business volatility comes from transaction size and resulting revenue and profit.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Price per Unit outliers = 81 records (0.84%), compared with Units Sold = 6.50%, Total Sales = 6.77%, Operating Profit = 7.32%.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 states that transaction scale dominates the dataset more than pricing variation.

title: Outlet sales naturally fit the broader volume-led revenue pattern
description: Across the full dataset, Units Sold and Total Sales are strongly correlated at 0.845, showing that revenue is primarily driven by transaction volume. Since Step 3 identifies this as a dominant structural relationship for the business, the Outlet channel is likely to follow the same pattern: larger outlet orders should systematically produce larger sales values, even if pricing remains relatively stable.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation between Units Sold and Total Sales = 0.844936119178483.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 states that units drive sales and that transaction scale is the dominant statistical pattern.

title: Amazon likely exhibits the same strong sales-to-profit linkage as the overall business
description: The overall relationship between Total Sales and Operating Profit is extremely strong at 0.956, making it one of the clearest structural patterns in the data. Because Step 3 identifies this linkage as business-wide and scale-driven, Amazon records are likely to show the same pattern: when Amazon sales rise on a transaction, operating profit usually rises with them in a closely aligned way.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit = 0.9563074748281619.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 describes sales-to-profit linkage as one of the strongest structural relationships in the dataset.
